title.en: High-Quality Sentence Embeddings & RAG Backend with all-mpnet-base-v2

description.en: Learn to generate high-quality 768-dimensional sentence embeddings with all-mpnet-base-v2 and wire them into a RAG retrieval backend. Covers encoding strategies, batch processing, semantic search indexing, and upgrading from weaker embedding models.

story_variants.en:
## High-Quality Sentence Embeddings & RAG Backend with all-mpnet-base-v2

Vaathiyaar here! You built a RAG pipeline last month and it works — mostly. But when a user asks "What are the side effects of ibuprofen?" your retriever returns a chunk about ibuprofen manufacturing instead of the safety data sheet. The problem isn't your chunking strategy or your vector database — it's your **embedding model**. Swap in `all-mpnet-base-v2` and watch retrieval precision jump overnight. This model produces 768-dimensional embeddings that score **0.838 on the STS benchmark** — the highest of any open-source sentence transformer — and it runs locally across four backends.

### Why Embedding Quality Is the RAG Bottleneck

RAG retrieval is only as good as the embeddings that power it. A weak embedding model collapses distinct meanings into nearby vectors, so your retriever returns plausible-looking but wrong chunks. The fix is straightforward: use a model that maps semantically different sentences to distant points in vector space.

| Model | Dims | STS Score | Max Tokens | RAG Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| all-mpnet-base-v2 | 768 | **0.838** | 384 | Best open-source |
| all-MiniLM-L6-v2 | 384 | 0.789 | 256 | Fast prototyping |
| text-embedding-ada-002 | 1536 | ~0.80 | 8191 | API-locked, costly |
| BGE-M3 | 1024 | ~0.83 | 8192 | Multilingual focus |

mpnet hits the sweet spot: best-in-class accuracy, moderate dimensionality, and zero API costs.

### Generating Embeddings for RAG

```python
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")

# Encode document chunks for your knowledge base
chunks = [
    "Ibuprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug used for pain relief.",
    "Common side effects include stomach pain, nausea, and dizziness.",
    "Ibuprofen is manufactured through a multi-step chemical synthesis.",
    "Do not exceed 1200mg per day without medical supervision.",
]

embeddings = model.encode(chunks, normalize_embeddings=True, show_progress_bar=True)
print(embeddings.shape)  # (4, 768)
print(np.linalg.norm(embeddings[0]))  # 1.0 — unit-length for dot-product search
```

`normalize_embeddings=True` is critical for RAG: it produces unit-length vectors so cosine similarity reduces to a dot product — the fastest operation every vector database supports.

### Building a Minimal Semantic Search Index

```python
class EmbeddingIndex:
    def __init__(self, model_name="all-mpnet-base-v2"):
        self.model = SentenceTransformer(model_name)
        self.texts = []
        self.embeddings = None

    def add(self, documents: list[str]):
        self.texts.extend(documents)
        new_embs = self.model.encode(documents, normalize_embeddings=True)
        if self.embeddings is None:
            self.embeddings = new_embs
        else:
            self.embeddings = np.vstack([self.embeddings, new_embs])

    def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 3) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
        q_emb = self.model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)
        scores = (self.embeddings @ q_emb.T).flatten()
        top_indices = scores.argsort()[::-1][:top_k]
        return [(self.texts[i], float(scores[i])) for i in top_indices]

index = EmbeddingIndex()
index.add(chunks)
results = index.search("What are the side effects?")
for text, score in results:
    print(f"{score:.3f} | {text[:60]}")
```

The matrix multiply `embeddings @ q_emb.T` computes all similarity scores in one vectorized operation. In production, replace this with FAISS or a managed vector database — but the embedding model stays the same.

### Batch Processing for Large Corpora

```python
def embed_corpus(texts: list[str], model, batch_size: int = 64) -> np.ndarray:
    all_embeddings = []
    for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size):
        batch = texts[i:i + batch_size]
        embs = model.encode(
            batch,
            normalize_embeddings=True,
            convert_to_numpy=True,
            show_progress_bar=False,
        )
        all_embeddings.append(embs)
    return np.vstack(all_embeddings)

# Process 10,000 chunks without OOM
large_corpus = ["Document chunk " + str(i) for i in range(10000)]
corpus_embeddings = embed_corpus(large_corpus, model, batch_size=128)
print(corpus_embeddings.shape)  # (10000, 768)
```

Batch processing avoids GPU out-of-memory errors and gives you a progress hook for logging. On a consumer GPU, 128-batch encoding processes ~1,000 sentences per second.

### Upgrading an Existing Pipeline

```python
# Before — MiniLM (384-dim, STS 0.789)
old_model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
old_emb = old_model.encode(["side effects of ibuprofen"])
print(old_emb.shape)  # (1, 384)

# After — mpnet (768-dim, STS 0.838)
new_model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")
new_emb = new_model.encode(["side effects of ibuprofen"])
print(new_emb.shape)  # (1, 768)

# NOTE: You must re-encode your entire corpus when switching models.
# Embeddings from different models are NOT compatible.
```

The upgrade is one line of code — but you **must re-embed your entire corpus**. Different models map to different vector spaces, so old embeddings cannot be mixed with new ones.

> **Key Insight:** all-mpnet-base-v2 is the highest-quality open-source embedding model for RAG retrieval — 768 dimensions, 0.838 STS score, zero API costs. The upgrade from MiniLM is one line of code, but the precision gain is measurable: fewer wrong chunks returned, fewer hallucinated answers from your LLM. Always use `normalize_embeddings=True` so your vector database can use fast dot-product search instead of cosine similarity.

animation_sequence.content: Your RAG pipeline retrieves the wrong chunks because your embedding model can't distinguish similar-looking but semantically different sentences. all-mpnet-base-v2 fixes this — 768 dimensions, 0.838 STS score, four deployment backends, zero API costs. One model swap upgrades your entire retrieval stack.

animation_sequence.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")

chunks = [
    "Ibuprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug used for pain relief.",
    "Common side effects include stomach pain, nausea, and dizziness.",
    "Ibuprofen is manufactured through a multi-step chemical synthesis.",
    "Do not exceed 1200mg per day without medical supervision.",
]

embeddings = model.encode(chunks, normalize_embeddings=True)
print(embeddings.shape)       # (4, 768)
print(np.linalg.norm(embeddings[0]))  # ~1.0

steps.explanation: Import SentenceTransformer — the library that wraps transformer models for producing fixed-size sentence embeddings from variable-length text.
Load all-mpnet-base-v2 — built on Microsoft's MPNet architecture, trained on 1B+ sentence pairs. Produces 768-dimensional vectors with the highest open-source STS benchmark score (0.838).
Four document chunks simulating a RAG knowledge base. A good embedding model must place the 'side effects' chunk far from the 'manufacturing' chunk despite both mentioning ibuprofen.
encode() converts each string into a 768-float vector. normalize_embeddings=True makes each vector unit-length — critical for fast dot-product search in vector databases.
Shape (4, 768): four chunks, each represented by 768 floats. This is your embedding matrix — ready to insert into FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, or any vector store.
Norm is 1.0 because we normalized. This means cosine_similarity(a, b) = dot(a, b) — the fastest similarity operation, natively supported by every vector database.

animation_sequence.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")

class EmbeddingIndex:
    def __init__(self, model):
        self.model = model
        self.texts = []
        self.embeddings = None

    def add(self, documents: list[str]):
        new_embs = self.model.encode(documents, normalize_embeddings=True)
        self.texts.extend(documents)
        if self.embeddings is None:
            self.embeddings = new_embs
        else:
            self.embeddings = np.vstack([self.embeddings, new_embs])

    def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 3):
        q_emb = self.model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)
        scores = (self.embeddings @ q_emb.T).flatten()
        top_idx = scores.argsort()[::-1][:top_k]
        return [(self.texts[i], float(scores[i])) for i in top_idx]

index = EmbeddingIndex(model)
index.add(["side effects include nausea", "manufactured via synthesis", "dosage is 400mg"])
results = index.search("What are the side effects?")
for text, score in results:
    print(f"{score:.3f} | {text}")

steps.explanation: A minimal semantic search index — the core of any RAG retrieval layer. It stores texts alongside their embeddings and supports similarity search.
add() encodes new documents and appends them. In production, you'd batch-insert into a vector database; here numpy arrays demonstrate the principle.
normalize_embeddings=True ensures all vectors are unit-length. This is set once during encoding and never needs to be repeated at search time.
np.vstack stacks new embeddings below existing ones. The embedding matrix grows as you add documents — row i corresponds to self.texts[i].
search() encodes the query with the same model and same normalization. Query and document embeddings must come from the same model — never mix models.
Matrix multiply embeddings @ q_emb.T computes all dot-product scores in one vectorized operation. On normalized vectors, this equals cosine similarity.
argsort()[::-1][:top_k] returns the indices of the highest-scoring documents. This is the retrieval step — the top-k chunks feed into your LLM's context window.
Build the index, add three chunks, and search. The 'side effects' chunk should score highest for the side effects query — demonstrating semantic understanding beyond keyword matching.

animation_sequence.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

def embed_corpus(texts, model, batch_size=64):
    all_embeddings = []
    for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size):
        batch = texts[i:i + batch_size]
        embs = model.encode(batch, normalize_embeddings=True)
        all_embeddings.append(embs)
    return np.vstack(all_embeddings)

# Upgrade path: MiniLM -> mpnet
old = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
new = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")

print(old.encode(["test"]).shape)  # (1, 384)
print(new.encode(["test"]).shape)  # (1, 768)

# IMPORTANT: re-embed entire corpus after switching models
corpus = [f"Document chunk {i}" for i in range(1000)]
new_embeddings = embed_corpus(corpus, new, batch_size=128)
print(new_embeddings.shape)  # (1000, 768)

steps.explanation: A batch encoding function for large corpora. Processing in batches prevents GPU OOM errors and lets you add progress logging between batches.
Iterate through the corpus in batch_size chunks. 64 is safe for most GPUs; increase to 128-256 if you have sufficient VRAM.
Each batch returns a numpy array of shape (batch_size, 768). normalize_embeddings=True is applied per-batch — normalization is per-vector, so batching doesn't affect results.
np.vstack concatenates all batch arrays into one (N, 768) matrix. This is your full corpus embedding — ready for indexing.
Load both models side by side. MiniLM is 80MB and fast; mpnet is 420MB but more accurate. The API is identical — only the model name changes.
MiniLM produces 384-dim vectors. mpnet produces 768-dim vectors. These live in different vector spaces and are NOT interchangeable — you cannot mix them in the same index.
After switching models, you must re-embed every document. This is a one-time cost that pays dividends in retrieval quality for every subsequent query.

animation_sequence.message: You can now generate high-quality 768-dimensional embeddings and wire them into a RAG backend! Your retriever will return the right chunks — not just plausible-looking ones — because mpnet understands meaning, not just keywords.

instruction.en: Build an `EmbeddingIndex` class that stores documents as normalized word-frequency vectors and supports semantic search via dot-product scoring. Implement `encode(text)` to return a word frequency dict (lowercase), `normalize(vec)` to return a unit-length copy, `add(documents)` to store texts and their normalized embeddings, and `search(query, top_k)` to return the top-k most similar documents as a list of `(text, score)` tuples sorted by score descending. This mirrors how all-mpnet-base-v2 powers RAG retrieval with normalized embeddings and dot-product search.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import math

class EmbeddingIndex:
    def __init__(self):
        self.texts = []
        self.embeddings = []  # list of normalized dicts

    def encode(self, text: str) -> dict:
        # Return word frequency vector: {word: count}
        # Lowercase all words
        pass

    def normalize(self, vec: dict) -> dict:
        # Return a unit-length copy of vec
        # magnitude = sqrt(sum of squares)
        # Return empty dict if magnitude is 0
        pass

    def add(self, documents: list[str]):
        # Encode each doc, normalize it, store text and embedding
        pass

    def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 3) -> list[tuple]:
        # 1. Encode and normalize the query
        # 2. Compute dot product with each stored embedding
        # 3. Return top_k results as [(text, score), ...] sorted by score desc
        pass

# Test it:
index = EmbeddingIndex()
index.add([
    "machine learning with python",
    "cooking italian pasta recipe",
    "python programming tutorial",
])
results = index.search("python machine learning", top_k=2)
print(len(results))
print(results[0][1] > results[1][1])

# Verify normalization produces unit vectors
vec = index.normalize({"a": 3, "b": 4})
magnitude = math.sqrt(sum(v**2 for v in vec.values()))
print(round(magnitude, 1))

en: encode: use text.lower().split() and count occurrences with a dict — {'word': count}
normalize: compute magnitude with math.sqrt(sum(v**2 for v in vec.values())), then return {k: v/magnitude for k, v in vec.items()}
search: dot product of two dicts is sum(q[k]*d[k] for k in shared keys). Sort results by score descending and slice [:top_k]

practice_challenges.test_code:
idx = EmbeddingIndex()
assert idx.encode("hello world") == {"hello": 1, "world": 1}
assert idx.encode("go go go") == {"go": 3}
vec = idx.normalize({"a": 3, "b": 4})
assert abs(math.sqrt(sum(v**2 for v in vec.values())) - 1.0) < 0.001
assert idx.normalize({}) == {}
idx.add(["a b c", "x y z", "a b x"])
assert len(idx.texts) == 3
assert len(idx.embeddings) == 3
results = idx.search("a b", top_k=2)
assert len(results) == 2
assert results[0][1] >= results[1][1]
assert isinstance(results[0], tuple)
assert isinstance(results[0][1], float)
results_all = idx.search("a b", top_k=10)
assert len(results_all) == 3

instruction.en: Build a `CorpusUpgrader` class that simulates upgrading a RAG pipeline from a low-dimensional embedding model to a higher-dimensional one. Implement `low_dim_encode(text)` returning a 2-key vector (first two unique words), `high_dim_encode(text)` returning the full word frequency vector, `compare_retrieval(query, docs)` that runs the query against both encodings and returns a dict with 'low_dim_top' and 'high_dim_top' (the best-matching document text for each), and `re_embed(docs)` that returns the full list of high-dim normalized embeddings. This demonstrates why re-embedding is mandatory when upgrading models.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
side effects include nausea and headache
1.0
1.0

en: low_dim_encode: split and lowercase, get unique words with dict.fromkeys() or a seen set, keep only the first 2, count their frequencies
compare_retrieval: normalize query and each doc embedding for both encodings, compute dot scores, pick the doc with the highest score for each
re_embed: call high_dim_encode on each doc, then _normalize each result, return the list
